narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at their academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and more to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 33, lower-middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and predicted a 61.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average—suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance as coursework intensified. Your Year 1 decile ranking, also at the bottom 10% versus the average around the middle, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest driver toward a failure prediction, indicating early academic hurdles that the model sees as persistent risks. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 acted as the third-most important factor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing success since it's close to the dataset average of 3.21, showing you have a solid foundation despite other dips. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your lower deciles stand out sharply, as most successful ones rank in the 5th or 6th decile, while your LSAT of 33 falls short of the average 36.7 seen in passers. Your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 90%) of students, so it didn't sway things much either way. This combination painted a picture of higher bar failure risk for you specifically, even with your determination evident in pursuing law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile rankings, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as an Asian male navigating these challenges. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor, and many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these areas. Keep pushing—you've got the drive, and targeted improvements can open those doors.
